---
title: "Literature Review & Evidence Synthesis"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
[Moderate]{.level-badge .level-moderate} · **Communication & Dissemination** track · ~1 hour, self-paced
:::

[← Back to the Communication & Dissemination track](communication.qmd){.track-back}

*Speed the mechanical parts of a review while keeping the judgment and the citations yours.*

## What you'll be able to do

- Use AI to draft search strategies and screening criteria
- Summarize and compare studies with verification
- Avoid fabricated citations and keep a defensible record

## Overview

AI can draft a search strategy, suggest inclusion criteria, and summarize what a set of papers says. It can also invent references that look real, so every citation must be checked against the actual paper.

For a defensible review, keep AI in an assisting role: it speeds screening and summarizing, but you decide what's included and confirm what each source actually says.

## Practice activities

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Activity 1 · Moderate — Draft a search strategy

1. Describe your question (PICO-style) to AI.
2. Ask for **search terms and a Boolean strategy** for one database.
3. Run it in the real database; refine the terms based on what returns.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Activity 2 · Moderate — Summarize and verify

1. Paste 2–3 **open-access** abstracts and ask AI to compare findings in a table.
2. Open each paper and confirm the summary is faithful — especially numbers.
3. Note any place the AI overstated or blurred a finding.
:::

## Check your readiness

Answer these, then check — your score suggests whether to dive in or skim the readings first.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions": [{"q": "The single biggest risk in AI-assisted reviews is…", "options": ["Fabricated or misattributed citations", "Slow searches", "Too many results", "Boring summaries"], "answer": 0, "explain": "If you can't open it, you can't cite it — verify every reference."}, {"q": "Who decides what's included in the review?", "options": ["You, against explicit criteria", "The AI", "The database", "Whoever has the login"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Inclusion is a judgment call you own and document."}, {"q": "AI summaries of papers should be…", "options": ["Checked against the source, especially numbers", "Trusted if fluent", "Pasted directly", "Ignored"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Fluency is not accuracy."}]}
</script>
</div>

## Recommended readings

From the AI readings library (ask the facilitator for access):

- **CoPaper.AI — Your AI Research Co-Author** — what AI research assistants can and can't do.
- **Read-Paper-Write-Code: Agentic Reproduction of Social-Science Results (Kohler et al.)** — AI reproducing analyses — promise and limits.

## Useful resources

- [PRISMA](https://www.prisma-statement.org) — reporting standard for systematic reviews.
- [Rayyan](https://www.rayyan.ai) — screening tool for reviews.
- [Covidence](https://www.covidence.org) — systematic-review management.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules
Use **public/open-access** sources for practice; verify every citation against the original. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
